skill.name: routeros-qemu-chr
skill.description: MikroTik RouterOS CHR (Cloud Hosted Router) with QEMU. Use when: running RouterOS in QEMU, booting CHR images, debugging CHR boot failures, setting up VirtIO devices for RouterOS, choosing between SeaBIOS and UEFI boot, configuring QEMU port forwarding for RouterOS REST API, or selecting QEMU acceleration (KVM/HVF/TCG).
skill.summary: Guide for running RouterOS CHR (Cloud Hosted Router) in QEMU with acceleration detection, VirtIO setup, and boot troubleshooting.
skill.author: tikoci

security_audit.summary: Static analysis detected 347 potential security patterns across 5 files. After semantic evaluation, all findings are FALSE POSITIVES. The skill uses external commands for QEMU invocation, which is the core purpose of this documentation. Network access is limited to mikrotik.com download URLs. No malicious intent or actual security vulnerabilities confirmed.

medium_findings.title: External Command Invocation in Documentation
medium_findings.description: Skill contains numerous shell commands and script examples for QEMU invocation. These are intentional documentation patterns, not security vulnerabilities. Commands are hardcoded with fixed arguments (qemu-system-x86_64, specific flags) and not subject to user input injection.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: All external command patterns are hardcoded in documentation examples for legitimate QEMU VM management. No user-controlled input flows into these commands.

medium_findings.title: System Device Access for Virtualization
medium_findings.description: Documentation references /dev/kvm access for KVM acceleration detection. This is standard practice for QEMU virtual machine management on Linux.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: /dev/kvm access is a documented requirement for hardware acceleration in QEMU. The skill explains the permission requirements and provides udev configuration patterns for CI environments.

low_findings.title: Historical Cryptographic Reference (MD5)
low_findings.description: MD5 is mentioned in virtio-drivers.md in the context of a kernel config option (CONFIG_BLK_DEV_NVME is unrelated to MD5). The chr-licensing.md references are in documentation explaining license expiry behavior, not for security purposes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: MD5 references in documentation are historical context or kernel config mentions, not active cryptographic usage in the skill.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Network URLs
low_findings.description: MikroTik download URLs (download.mikrotik.com, cdn.mikrotik.com) are hardcoded for CHR image downloads. These are official MikroTik infrastructure URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Hardcoded URLs point to official MikroTik download servers. No data exfiltration risk as the skill only reads from these endpoints.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance for Acceleration Detection
low_findings.description: The skill checks for KVM availability (/dev/kvm) and HVF support (sysctl kern.hv_support) to determine appropriate QEMU acceleration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Acceleration detection is standard practice for QEMU virtual machine setup. The checks verify hardware capabilities before choosing between KVM, HVF, or TCG.

low_findings.title: Sudo Usage in CI Workflows
low_findings.description: GitHub Actions CI documentation uses sudo for apt-get installation and udev configuration. This is standard CI practice.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Sudo usage in CI documentation follows standard GitHub Actions patterns for installing packages and configuring device permissions.

content.user_title: Run MikroTik RouterOS CHR in QEMU
content.value_statement: Running RouterOS CHR in QEMU requires understanding boot methods, VirtIO drivers, and acceleration options. This skill provides guidance for x86_64 and aarch64 architectures with troubleshooting for common issues.
actual_capabilities: Download RouterOS CHR images from official MikroTik servers (stable, testing, development, long-term channels)
Configure QEMU for x86_64 (SeaBIOS) and aarch64 (UEFI) boot with appropriate firmware
Set up VirtIO devices (block, network, serial) with correct device syntax for each architecture
Detect hardware acceleration (KVM on Linux, HVF on macOS) and fall back to TCG emulation
Configure port forwarding for RouterOS services (WebFig/REST on port 80, SSH on port 22, API on port 8728)
Implement health checks and boot wait loops for automated testing workflows
limitations: QEMU Guest Agent (QGA) requires KVM; it does not start under HVF or TCG acceleration
The check-installation command fails on aarch64 due to firmware/DTB limitations that cannot be resolved
Cross-architecture TCG (x86_64 guest on aarch64 host) is not viable due to I/O port emulation constraints

use_cases.title: Set up a RouterOS testing VM in CI/CD
use_cases.description: Configure a CHR instance in GitHub Actions with KVM acceleration for schema generation, API testing, or integration tests against RouterOS REST API.
use_cases.target_user: DevOps engineers and CI/CD pipeline developers

use_cases.title: Debug RouterOS boot failures on aarch64
use_cases.description: Identify and resolve VirtIO device issues, firmware path problems, or acceleration configuration problems when running RouterOS on Apple Silicon or ARM servers.

use_cases.title: Develop RouterOS automation scripts
use_cases.description: Boot a CHR instance and interact with the REST API or CLI to test automation scripts, configuration templates, or container deployments.
use_cases.target_user: Network automation developers and DevNet engineers

prompt_templates.title: Start a basic CHR instance
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I start a RouterOS CHR instance with QEMU on Linux with KVM acceleration?
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants to boot RouterOS for basic testing or development

prompt_templates.title: Configure port forwarding for REST API
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up QEMU port forwarding so I can access RouterOS WebFig on localhost:9180 and SSH on localhost:9122.
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs network access to RouterOS services from the host

prompt_templates.title: Fix aarch64 boot failure with VirtIO
prompt_templates.prompt: My RouterOS CHR on aarch64 hangs during boot when using if=virtio. How do I fix this?
prompt_templates.scenario: User encounters the if=virtio trap on ARM architecture

prompt_templates.title: Set up CI pipeline with CHR
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a GitHub Actions workflow that downloads RouterOS CHR, boots it with KVM, waits for the REST API to be ready, and runs API tests.
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants automated testing with RouterOS

output_examples.input: How do I start a CHR instance with KVM on Linux?
output_examples.output: Use the -accel kvm flag with qemu-system-x86_64. Check /dev/kvm is writable first with [ -w /dev/kvm ]. Example: qemu-system-x86_64 -M q35 -m 256 -accel kvm -drive file=chr.img,format=raw,if=virtio -netdev user,id=net0,hostfwd=tcp::9180-:80 -device virtio-net-pci,netdev=net0

output_examples.input: Why does my aarch64 CHR fail to boot with VirtIO?
output_examples.output: On aarch64, if=virtio resolves to MMIO transport, but RouterOS has no virtio-mmio driver. Use explicit PCI device: -drive file=chr.img,format=raw,if=none,id=drive0 -device virtio-blk-pci,drive=drive0. This works on both x86_64 and aarch64.

output_examples.input: How do I detect if KVM is available from a script?
output_examples.output: On Linux, check [ -w /dev/kvm ] to verify KVM device is accessible. On macOS, run sysctl -n kern.hv_support and check for '1'. Both require host/guest architecture match (x64 guest on x64 host, or arm64 guest on arm64 host).

best_practices: Use explicit -device virtio-blk-pci instead of if=virtio shorthand to ensure compatibility across architectures
Implement health checks on /rest/system/resource (not just /) when immediately calling the REST API after boot
Always include the udev rule for /dev/kvm permissions in CI environments, even if it is a no-op on some runners
anti_patterns: Do not use if=virtio on aarch64; it resolves to MMIO and causes silent boot hangs
Do not specify CPU models (like cortex-a710) when using HVF acceleration on Apple Silicon; use -cpu host instead
Do not skip the health check and immediately call REST API; the startup has multiple stages (connection refused, then HTTP up, then REST ready)

faq.question: What is the free CHR license limit?
faq.answer: The free CHR license limits interface throughput to 1 Mbps. API calls, SSH, and management traffic are unaffected. This is sufficient for development, testing, and API work.

faq.question: How do I enable the 60-day trial license?
faq.answer: Create a free mikrotik.com account, then run /system/license/renew account=<email> password=<password> level=p-unlimited in the CHR. The trial activates immediately with P-Unlimited speeds.

faq.question: Why does QEMU Guest Agent not start?
faq.answer: RouterOS QGA requires KVM hardware acceleration. Under HVF (macOS) or TCG (software emulation), the daemon does not start because CPUID does not report KVM vendor strings. Use Linux with KVM for QGA functionality.

faq.question: Can I run x86_64 RouterOS on Apple Silicon Mac?
faq.answer: Yes, via TCG software emulation (not Rosetta). Use the aarch64 CHR image instead: qemu-system-aarch64 -M virt -cpu cortex-a72. x86_64 guest on aarch64 host is not viable due to I/O port emulation limitations.

faq.question: What is the difference between SeaBIOS and UEFI boot?
faq.answer: SeaBIOS handles the standard CHR image with its proprietary boot sector via MBR chain-load. UEFI requires the fat-chr repackaged image with FAT16 EFI partition. Use SeaBIOS for x86_64 (faster), UEFI for aarch64 or special cases.

faq.question: How do I handle multiple CHR instances?
faq.answer: Use unique host ports per instance (9180, 9181, 9182 for WebFig/REST; 9122, 9123, 9124 for SSH). Each instance needs its own QEMU process and disk image. The free CHR license allows unlimited instances.
